title: Chef Jon Kung on the Kitchen Gear They Swear By—and the One Invention They’re Still Waiting For
summary: When chef Jon Kung was a broke line cook, they spotted an AGA induction range in a local chef’s kitchen and made a vow: “As soon as I make it, make it, that’s going to be the first thing that I buy.” Years later, after TikTok fame and a cookbook deal, they kept that promise. Now the 42-year-old Chinese American creator, known for their third-culture feasts that span mapo tofu to Cantonese roast duck, is pulling back the curtain on their kitchen obsessions—and one enduring regret. “In my early twenties, I bought this used SMEG fridge,” Kung tells WIRED. “It’s got this giant British flag on it, and I still have it. I’ve stuck Sex Pistols, Ozzy Osbourne, and Spice Girls stickers on the sides to try to make it a little better.” The fridge has become a conversation piece at dinner parties, but Kung’s real kitchen fantasy is far more practical: “A rice cooker that also washes your rice.” The reason is both culinary and slightly horrifying. “It’s so important to wash your rice, especially if you’re making Asian rice,” they explain. “Also, I don’t think people know there are bug eggs in rice. They’re called rice weevils, and unless you’re buying that super expensive prewashed rice, there are lots of bugs in rice.” For now, Kung swears by Zojirushi rice cookers with pressure options, which keep rice fresh for an astonishingly long time. Kung’s advice on pans is refreshingly blunt: avoid combination nonstick-and-stainless pans. “They just end up being garbage versions for both jobs,” they say. Instead, invest in one ceramic nonstick pan (they recommend GreenPan with a three-ply stainless steel bottom) for delicate foods like eggs, and one stainless steel pan (Demeyere is their go-to) for searing and frying. The priciest item in their kitchen is that AGA Total Control induction range, which they prefer over gas for its lack of toxic fumes, easier cleaning, and better performance. On knives, Kung is equally practical: buy two affordable knives, like Victorinox, instead of one expensive blade. “You can have the fanciest knife in the world, but if you don’t get it sharpened, there’s no point—ha!” They recommend mail-in sharpening services, noting that Williams Sonoma’s Knife Aid partnership sharpens five knives for $69—just $13.80 per knife. And if you’re lucky, you might even find a converted ice cream truck that sharpens knives in Brooklyn, much to the confusion of local kids expecting a sweet treat.
why_it_matters: Kung’s advice demystifies kitchen tools for a generation that cooks at home more than ever, showing how smart purchases can save money and improve food safety. The dream of a rice-washing cooker also speaks to a broader hunger for appliances that respect diverse culinary traditions—not just Western ones—and push companies to innovate for the multicultural kitchens that millions of families actually use every day.

keywords.explanation: A type of stove or range that heats pans directly using magnetic fields instead of a flame or electric coil.
A term describing people who grew up in a culture different from their parents' original culture, often blending identities.
Small beetles that lay eggs in stored grains like rice, often unnoticed until they hatch.
A type of nonstick coating made from sand-derived materials, often used on pans as a safer alternative to traditional nonstick chemicals.
Made of three layers of metal bonded together, commonly used in cookware to improve heat distribution.
In cooking, the use of sealed, high-pressure steam to cook food faster; some rice cookers have this option.

questions.question: What was the first big kitchen purchase Jon Kung made after finding success?
Which stickers did Kung put on their SMEG fridge to make it look better?
options: Sex Pistols, Ozzy Osbourne, and Spice Girls stickers
questions.correct_answer: Sex Pistols, Ozzy Osbourne, and Spice Girls stickers

questions.question: What kitchen invention does Kung want that doesn't yet exist?
options: A rice cooker that also washes rice
questions.correct_answer: A rice cooker that also washes rice

questions.question: Why does Kung emphasize the importance of washing rice?
options: To remove excess starch for fluffier rice.
Because unwashed rice can contain bug eggs called rice weevils.
Because it's a Japanese cultural tradition.
To make the rice cook faster.
questions.correct_answer: Because unwashed rice can contain bug eggs called rice weevils.

questions.question: What kind of pan does Kung strongly advise against buying?
How much does a mail-in knife sharpening cost per knife through Williams Sonoma's partnership?

background_read: Jon Kung is a Chinese American chef who grew up between cultures (hence the term 'third-culture') and built a large following on TikTok by blending Chinese dishes like mapo tofu with Western comfort food, eventually publishing a cookbook.

AGA, a British brand founded in 1922, is famous for its heavy cast-iron stoves that stay on all day; their newer induction ranges use magnetic technology that is more energy-efficient and doesn’t produce combustion fumes.
SMEG is an Italian appliance maker known for colorful, retro-style refrigerators that have become design icons; the British-flag fridge Kung owns is a limited-edition model.

Zojirushi is a Japanese company originally founded in 1918 that is highly regarded for its rice cookers, especially those with fuzzy logic and pressure-cooking features that can keep rice fresh for hours.

Rice weevils (Sitophilus oryzae) are a common pest worldwide; their eggs are laid inside grains and can hatch weeks later, which is why traditional rice preparation in many Asian cultures includes thorough washing.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Jon Kung made a vow as a broke line cook to buy an AGA induction range—and kept that promise after success.
QUIRKY CONFESSION: The SMEG fridge with a British flag and punk/pop stickers has become a conversation starter.
KITCHEN WISH LIST: Kung’s dream appliance is a rice cooker that also washes rice.
├─ REASON: Unwashed rice can contain rice weevil eggs.
└─ CURRENT GEAR: Recommends Zojirushi rice cookers with pressure options.
PAN PHILOSOPHY: Avoid 2-in-1 nonstick/stainless pans; invest in separate ceramics (GreenPan) and stainless steel (Demeyere).
HEAT SOURCE: Prefers induction over gas for health and cleaning benefits.
KNIFE STRATEGY: Buy two affordable knives (Victorinox) and use mail-in sharpening services.
└─ COST: Williams Sonoma’s Knife Aid partnership sharpens five knives for $69, about $13.80 each.
FINAL WHIMSY: A Brooklyn ice cream truck that sharpens knives instead of selling treats.

perspectives.description: Kung’s unfiltered guidance helps home cooks avoid wasting money on trendy but lousy equipment. By naming specific brands and realistic budgets (like two Victorinox knives instead of one luxury blade), they make good cooking accessible, not pretentious.

Not everyone can afford an AGA induction range or high-end Demeyere pans; Kung’s recommendations, while practical, still assume a certain level of disposable income. This highlights a persistent inequality in the food world where quality gear is often a privilege, not a given.

The article reflects a wider shift in how kitchen knowledge is shared: from professional chef to mass audience through social media. TikTok creators like Kung have replaced traditional cooking shows as the primary source of gear advice for younger generations, fundamentally altering the relationship between brands and consumers.

Kung’s wish for a rice cooker that washes rice points to the future of smart kitchen appliances. As multicultural diets become mainstream, manufacturers may finally design appliances that automate steps like washing grains or adjusting for specific rice varieties, making home cooking less intimidating and more globally attuned.
